original_text: The global economy experiences a significant recession due to a pandemic.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The global economy collapses into a catastrophic depression due to a pandemic, with GDP contracting by over 20%, mass unemployment, and a recovery timeline spanning more than a decade.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The global economy falls into a deep and prolonged recession due to a pandemic, with widespread business failures, surging unemployment, and years of economic stagnation.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The global economy experiences a severe recession due to a pandemic, with sharp GDP declines across most sectors and a slow, uneven recovery.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The global economy experiences a significant recession due to a pandemic.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The global economy experiences a moderate recession due to a pandemic, with noticeable but manageable economic contraction and a relatively steady recovery.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The global economy experiences a mild recession due to a pandemic, with a brief period of reduced growth before returning to expansion.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The global economy experiences only a minor and temporary economic slowdown due to a pandemic, with a slight dip in output quickly followed by a robust rebound.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario — a pandemic affecting the global economy — but varies the severity of the economic downturn. Tier 1 depicts a catastrophic depression with decade-long recovery, while Tier 7 depicts a minor, temporary slowdown with a quick rebound. Each adjacent tier represents a meaningful but graduated step in severity, and every rational agent would prefer less severe economic disruption.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a minor temporary slowdown with a quick rebound causes the least harm to livelihoods, businesses, and societal well-being, whereas deeper recessions and depressions produce widespread suffering, unemployment, and long-term damage.
